source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
In the standard second-order system shown in <image>, with poles \( p \) and \( p^* \) having negative real parts, consider two such second-order systems:

System 1: \( \omega_n = 3 \, \text{rad/sec} \) and \( \theta = 60^\circ \) 
System 2: \( \omega_n = 1 \, \text{rad/sec} \) and \( \theta = 70^\circ \)

Which one of the following statements is correct?
source_intake_record.raw_answer_text: Settling time of System 2 is more than that of System 1.

choice_map.A: Settling time of System 1 is more than that of System 2.
choice_map.B: Settling time of System 2 is more than that of System 1.
choice_map.C: Settling times of both systems are the same.
choice_map.D: Settling time cannot be computed from the given information.

extraction_notes: image field is a PIL image object placeholder, not a path or URL

image_dependency: question_text explicitly says 'shown in the figure'
the meaning of angle theta appears tied to the standard pole-location diagram, so the figure likely helps define the geometry
multi_step: solving requires relating second-order pole parameters (omega_n, theta) to a time-domain quantity (settling time)
the task involves comparing two systems rather than reading off a single value
the answer requires at least a parameter-to-formula mapping step and a comparison step
multiple-choice structure with 4 options suggests evaluable reasoning over alternatives
verifiability: answer_text is a clear discrete claim matching one of the provided choices
registry_passed=true, so core assets are available for later checking
the result can be checked against standard second-order-system relations if the figure's theta convention is confirmed

decision_reasons: Core assets are complete: question, answer, and primary image are all present, and registry_passed is true.
Initial scores are solid for candidate value, with high image dependency and good multi-step and verifiability scores.
The sample appears recoverable and checkable because the answer is short text and tied to a discrete choice claim.
There is a noted risk that diagram convention may affect theta interpretation, but this is a follow-up concern rather than a core-asset failure.
